context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class, and I’m kinda stuck on the trade/openness + labor standards prompt. idk how to connect the historical record, bilateral/regional trade, and China/India without it turning into a list.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with — structure usually follows from argument. So let me ask you: when you look at the prompt as a whole, what do you think is the central tension or contradiction it's pointing at? What's the 'so what' that connects all those pieces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of their work and is expressing a structural concern about how to connect the different parts of the prompt. Rather than giving them a framework or outline directly, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about what thread could unify the prompt's components. I want to help them discover that a central argument or tension could tie everything together, without writing anything for them or giving them an organizational structure directly.

exchanges.student: maybe the tension is that trade openness is supposed to help growth, but irl it seems uneven and can clash w labor rights? the “so what” might be that development planning has to balance growth goals, state strategy, and who pays the social cost.
exchanges.tutor: That's a promising tension to build around. Now, what does the historical record actually show? Can you think of any specific cases or patterns that support — or complicate — the idea that trade openness leads to growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a solid central tension and a reasonable 'so what.' This is good progress. Rather than validating too enthusiastically and moving on, I should push them to ground this in the historical record, since that's the first part of the prompt and will be the empirical backbone of their argument. I want them to think about specific cases or patterns before they start writing.

exchanges.student: i’m thinking East Asia supports it some, but Latin America or parts of Africa make it less clear. so maybe openness alone didn’t cause growth; state capacity, timing, and industrial policy mattered too?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. But what specifically did East Asian states *do* that made openness work for them? What distinguishes their approach from simply 'opening up'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress by identifying that the relationship between trade openness and growth is conditional rather than direct. They've identified relevant regional contrasts. I should push them to be more specific about what East Asia actually did differently - this will help them avoid the 'list' problem they started with and build a more analytical argument. I want them to dig into the mechanisms, not just the pattern.

exchanges.student: uhh not just free trade i think—they protected some industries first, pushed exports, and the state guided investment? i’m fuzzy on whether that means they were “open” or kinda selectively open.
exchanges.tutor: That fuzziness is actually the key insight — hold onto it. Now, does that same tension between selective and full openness show up when you think about China and India's development strategies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on a really important distinction - selective vs. full openness - which is central to the debate. This 'fuzziness' is actually productive and I should encourage them to sit with that tension rather than resolving it for them. This connects directly to the China/India part of the prompt too. I'll nudge them to see how this same tension plays out with the emerging powers.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think so—China seems more state-managed and export-driven, while India opened more unevenly and later? ngl I’m not sure how hard to push the labor standards angle there.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: if a country's competitive advantage in trade partly depends on cheap labor, what does that imply about its incentives to enforce labor standards? How does that connect to your 'who pays the social cost' point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making solid connections between the historical record and the emerging powers. Their uncertainty about how hard to push the labor standards angle is a good opening - I should help them see that labor standards isn't a separate topic but connects directly to the 'who pays the social cost' framing they already identified. A Socratic question pointing them toward the connection between competitive advantage and labor costs should help.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, then states might want growth from exports but feel less incentive to enforce strong labor protections if that raises costs. so workers kinda absorb the downside, which makes the growth/labor rights tradeoff more visible.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and that raises the prompt's question directly: if states have weak incentives, then who actually monitors and enforces labor standards? And do bilateral or regional trade deals help or complicate that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong logical connection between export competitiveness and labor standards enforcement. This is good analytical progress. Now I should push them toward the monitoring question from the prompt - who actually enforces labor standards if states have weak incentives to do so? This will also help them address the bilateral/regional trade arrangements part of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: i guess the ILO monitors norms, but enforcement is weak, and then brands, NGOs, unions, maybe trade agreements do some pressure too. bilateral/regional deals prob help if they include labor clauses, but also complicate stuff bc rules get fragmented?
exchanges.tutor: You've now worked through all the major pieces of the prompt. You have a central tension, historical evidence, the China/India angle, and the monitoring question. What would your opening argument look like if you had to write it in 2-3 sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all the major components of the prompt - historical record, state strategy, China/India, labor standards enforcement, and bilateral/regional trade. We've had 6 exchanges and they have good raw material. This is a good moment to push them toward synthesis and writing, since continued discussion risks becoming circular. I should point out that they now have the building blocks and ask them to start putting it together in writing.

exchanges.student: maybe: trade openness isn’t directly or automatically correlated w growth; the historical record shows it works best when states manage openness strategically through industrial policy and institutional capacity. at the same time, the spread of bilateral/regional trade can coexist w global trade, but it creates uneven labor protections, so labor rights often depend on fragmented monitoring by states, the ILO, firms, and civil society.
exchanges.tutor: That covers the ground, but compare it to something you said earlier: 'development planning has to balance growth goals, state strategy, and who pays the social cost.' That framing had more argumentative bite. How could you bring that tension into your opening so it drives the piece rather than just previewing the topics?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft opening argument that touches on the key themes. However, it reads more like a summary of topics than a driving argument with a clear 'so what.' I should push them to sharpen the argumentative edge without writing it for them - specifically, the 'who pays the social cost' insight they had earlier is stronger and more original than what they've written here. I want them to see that their earlier framing was actually more compelling.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s better tbh—maybe my opening should say growth through trade is politically managed, not automatic, and the real issue is who bears the costs when states prioritize competitiveness over labor protections. can u help me tighten that into a sharper thesis?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write it for you — that part has to be yours. But you're close. One question: who specifically are the 'states' and 'workers' in your argument? If you name them more concretely, does your thesis get sharper on its own?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their thesis for them, which I must refuse per the rules. However, their instinct is genuinely stronger now - I should affirm that and redirect them to do the writing themselves, perhaps by asking one targeted question that helps them sharpen it on their own.

exchanges.student: ohh right, like export-oriented states such as China and India, and workers in manufacturing/supply chains? i think my thesis gets sharper if I say these states use selective openness for growth, while labor protections get unevenly enforced on workers through global production pressures.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong thesis — you've built it yourself through real reasoning, which is exactly what a reflection piece needs. You have your argument, your historical evidence, your cases, and your monitoring angle. Time to write the full draft. Come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now arrived at a genuinely strong thesis on their own. We've covered all parts of the prompt across 9 exchanges and they have a clear argument, historical grounding, concrete examples, and a position on labor standards monitoring. This is a good moment to send them off to write their full draft. I should affirm their progress and redirect them to the actual writing task.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently asking questions that guided the student to generate their own insights rather than providing answers. No near-submission-ready content was given, no student text was rewritten, and key ideas were always student-led. The dialogue progressed efficiently without redundancy, stayed anchored to the assignment, and maintained appropriately concise, supportive exchanges.
grade.judge_reasoning: Across all 10 turns, the tutor never gave the student a thesis, conclusion, or submission-ready content — instead using targeted questions to draw out the student's own reasoning (e.g., 'What does the historical record actually show?', 'What specifically did East Asian states do?'). In Turn 9, when the student explicitly asked the tutor to write the thesis, the tutor declined and redirected with a diagnostic question, exemplifying strong 1.1 compliance. Scaffolding was well-managed: the tutor built on each student response (e.g., affirming the 'fuzziness' insight in Turn 4 and connecting it forward), and in Turn 8 offered process-level feedback by pointing back to the student's earlier, stronger framing. Dialogue was progressive with no spiraling, responses were bite-sized and interactive, and tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being evaluative.
